coredata.dc:title: Micro-electrical discharge coating of AlCoCrFeNi high entropy alloy on MgAz31B to enhance the corrosion and wear resistance

coredata.dc:description: The present research aims to enhance the performance of magnesium alloy (MgAZ31b) in corrosive environments by utilizing high entropy alloy (HEA) coatings, specifically focusing on AlCoCrFeNi. The process involves fabrication of micro-HEA tool by powder metallurgy process and coating preparation by using the fabricated micro tool in electrical discharge coating (EDC) process. The XRD analysis of prepared coating reveals the presence of both body-centered cubic (BCC) and face-centered cubic (FCC) phases. The key findings suggest that the AlCoCrFeNi HEA coating significantly impacts both the corrosion resistance and wear resistance properties of magnesium alloys. Among the different samples, coating prepared at optimum pulse discharge duration (Ton = 400μs) emerges as particularly effective, demonstrating a lowest corrosion penetration rate (0.16 mm/year) during electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) testing compared to the other coated and uncoated magnesium substrate (15.48 mm/year). Moreover, wear tests indicate promising results, with coated surfaces exhibiting improved wear rate (from 0.000145 g/N-m g/N-m to 0.0013 g/N-m) and reduced risk of tribological properties when used in implant applications. The increase in 3D surface roughness after coating was observed which aligns with Wenzel's model, indicating that higher roughness increases the contact angle. The HEA coating with higher 3D roughness than Mg surface exhibits higher contact angle of 95.8° compared to 58.7° for the Mg workpiece. The hydrophobic nature of coating can be advantageous for various biological functions, particularly in protein interactions. Thus, employing high entropy alloy coatings on magnesium alloys holds significant promise for optimizing their corrosion resistance properties, and make more suitable for implant applications.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The present research aims to enhance the performance of magnesium alloy (MgAZ31b) in corrosive environments by utilizing high entropy alloy (HEA) coatings, specifically focusing on AlCoCrFeNi. The process involves fabrication of micro-HEA tool by powder metallurgy process and coating preparation by using the fabricated micro tool in electrical discharge coating (EDC) process. The XRD analysis of prepared coating reveals the presence of both body-centered cubic (BCC) and face-centered cubic (FCC) phases. The key findings suggest that the AlCoCrFeNi HEA coating significantly impacts both the corrosion resistance and wear resistance properties of magnesium alloys. Among the different samples, coating prepared at optimum pulse discharge duration (Ton = 400μs) emerges as particularly effective, demonstrating a lowest corrosion penetration rate (0.16 mm/year) during electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) testing compared to the other coated and uncoated magnesium substrate (15.48 mm/year). Moreover, wear tests indicate promising results, with coated surfaces exhibiting improved wear rate (from 0.000145 g/N-m g/N-m to 0.0013 g/N-m) and reduced risk of tribological properties when used in implant applications. The increase in 3D surface roughness after coating was observed which aligns with Wenzel's model, indicating that higher roughness increases the contact angle. The HEA coating with higher 3D roughness than Mg surface exhibits higher contact angle of 95.8° compared to 58.7° for the Mg workpiece. The hydrophobic nature of coating can be advantageous for various biological functions, particularly in protein interactions. Thus, employing high entropy alloy coatings on magnesium alloys holds significant promise for optimizing their corrosion resistance properties, and make more suitable for implant applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Mg alloys have gained attention in biomedical implants due to their excellent biocompatibility and biodegradability [1,2]. However, their high degradation rate and production of hydrogen gas pose challenges [3]. Indeed, the extreme susceptibility to galvanic corrosion poses a significant limitation to the wide application of magnesium (Mg) alloys. Galvanic corrosion occurs when two dissimilar metals are in contact in the presence of an electrolyte, leading to accelerated corrosion of the less noble metal (in this case, magnesium). Surface modification techniques, such as applying protective coatings, aim to minimize degradation and control the release of by-products while maintaining mechanical properties [4–6]. Various methods, including anodization, chemical conversion coatings, plasma electrolytic oxidation (PEO), vapor deposition methods, laser cladding, ion plating, and magnetron sputtering, are used for coating formation [5,7]. Each method has its advantages and limitations in terms of cost, complexity, environmental impact, and coating properties. EDC is highlighted as a cost-effective and versatile method for coating various materials, including Mg alloys and HEAs. It offers advantages such as no requirement for high capital investment, skilled manpower, toxic chemicals, or environmental concerns [8–10]. EDC can produce thin and thick coatings with tailored properties suitable for biomedical applications. Studies have shown the potential of EDC coatings in improving corrosion resistance, tribological properties, and antibacterial properties of biomedical alloys [11]. Tyagi et al. 2022 carried out EDC to prepare the solid lubricant coating which resulted into excellent corrosion and wear resistance [9]. A low wettability of workpiece was achieved after EDC that shows the process potential biomedical applications [8]. Iman et al. 2021 deposited GO/RGO/TiC/TiB2 coating on biomedical Ti alloy via EDC and reported lowest coefficient of friction 0.35 after coating. Further, the corrosion properties improved nineteen times than Ti workpiece [12]. Maddu et al. 2022 obtained antibacterial properties on Ti alloy in EDC via brass tool [13]. Similarly, Elaiyarasan et al. (2021) carried out EDC on Mg alloy using a tungsten carbide-copper (WC–Cu) tool which demonstrated the role of current in improving the coating layer topography [14]. However, researchers could not explore EDC onto Mg alloys for its biomedical applications by evaluating the factors such as corrosion resistance, mechanical properties, wettability and surface topography. Also, limited research has been conducted on EDC of HEAs on Mg biomedical alloys, indicating an area for further exploration. HEAs are highlighted as promising materials for surface modification and coating in the biomedical field due to their unique properties, including wear resistance, corrosion resistance, hardness, and resistance to diffusion [15–18]. HEAs offer versatility in surface chemistry and elemental compositions, making them suitable for enhancing metallic biomaterials' functionality and longevity in medical implants [15,16]. High entropy alloys (HEAs) indeed exhibit intriguing properties owing to their high entropy effect [19,20]. Unlike traditional alloys or intermetallic compounds, HEAs contain multiple elements in roughly equal proportions, which creates a highly disordered atomic structure, leading to a high mixing entropy [16]. This high entropy effect favors the formation of solid solution phases rather than intermetallic compounds [21]. In practical terms, this means that when HEAs are used as coatings, they tend to inhibit the formation of intermetallic compounds and instead promote elemental mixing within the alloy [22]. Nobel et al. coated AlCoCrFeNi high entropy alloy on the SS316L steel through plasma discharge spraying at ambient condition to boost durability in erosive environments, further annealing is done for homogeneous heat distribution which enhances the hardness of coating [23]. Bello et al. prepared (AlCoCrFeNiSi)100-x(WC)x (x = 0, 20 and 40 wt%) high entropy alloy (HEA) in which effect of varying concentration of (WC)x coatings via high-velocity arc spraying was observed. They highlighted the coatings’ transition to a dual-phase structure (FCC, BCC, and carbides) and their improved corrosion resistance [24]. Zhang et al. reported the preparation of CoCrFeNi HEA using electrodeposition method, they observed that addition of GO not effected the crystal structure of coated surface but improve the forming quality as well as wear resistance [25]. As a result, the predominant phases found in HEA coatings are typically simple cubic solid solutions, with only a small number of precipitated phases. This can have significant implications for the properties and performance of the coatings, as the absence of intermetallic compounds can lead to improved mechanical properties, corrosion resistance, and other desirable characteristics [26,27]. However, aforementioned literature shows that less attention has been given to AlCoCrFeNi HEA to enhance the corrosion and wear resistance of magnesium alloy.

The paper focuses on utilizing EDC to prepare HEA coatings composed of aluminum (Al), cobalt (Co), chromium (Cr), iron (Fe), and nickel (Ni) for biomedical applications [28]. The study aims to understand the microstructure evolution and corrosion behavior of these coatings, identifying optimal machine parameters and their effects on coating topography and corrosion resistance. In summary, the research explores the potential of EDC for forming HEA coatings on metallic biomaterials, contributing to advancements in surface modification techniques for biomedical applications.

Magnesium alloy (Mg) AZ31B (dimension 3 × 3 × 0.2 cm), procured from Parshwamani Metals, Delhi. Powders of aluminum (Al), cobalt (Co), chromium (Cr), iron (Fe), and nickel (Ni) with 99.9 % purity were selected for coating. These powders were purchased from Savraj Traders. The mean average particles size distribution is calculated through image J software. The detail of physical structure like (shapes and particle sizes) of these powders are illustrated in Fig. 1 which shows that mean average particle size varies from 89 to 130 μm with Al of 127 μm, Co of 112.61 μm, Cr of 122.59 μm Fe of 120 μm, Ni of 130.59 μm which gets reduced to 89.18 μm for HEA powder. Scanning Electron Microscope (SEM) images and Energy Dispersive X-ray Spectroscopy (EDS) data of the Mg alloy are provided. Fig. 1 shows the SEM images of powders and MgAZ31b sheet. Elemental composition of Mg sheet obtained by EDS is given by table in Fig. 1 (g).

A Retsch 400 planetary ball mill was utilized for the milling process. An equal atomic proportion of AlCoCrFeNi alloy powder (20 % each element) was mixed in a tungsten mixing jar. This ensures that all components of the alloy are evenly distributed. Tungsten milling balls, 5 mm in diameter, were added to the mixture in a weight ratio of 10:1 (powder to balls). These balls aid in the mixing and grinding process to achieve homogeneity. To prevent excessive cold welding of the powder composites during milling, stearic acid was included in the powder mixtures at a concentration of 1 wt%. This additive helps reduce the likelihood of particles sticking together. Argon gas was used to provide an inert milling environment. This prevents oxidation of the alloy powder during the milling process, which could potentially alter its properties. The ball mill operated at 250 rpm, and milling was carried out for 28 h. These parameters were selected based on previous results to effectively disperse the powder [29]. After 6-h ball mill, powder particles maintained their original shape and uniformly dispersed. Therefore, mixing of the powders was carried out for 28 h, after which the mean particle size gets reduced and agglomerated HEA particle is obtained. After milling, samples were collected from the jars and it passed to the sieved to separate the powder from the milling balls.

A die made of H13 die steel material is utilized along with a punch to compress the powder. The entire compaction process is conducted on a Universal Testing Machine (UTM). This machine provides controlled application of force, allowing for precise compaction of the powder. During the compaction process, a load of 20 tons is applied for a duration of 2 min. The green pellets are sintered in a horizontal tube furnace. The ends of the furnace tube are connected to gas flow pipes via flanges. One side of the tube is connected to an argon gas cylinder, while the other is connected to a conical flask filled with water. This setup helps control the gas flow and maintain the desired atmosphere within the furnace. The furnace temperature is increased at a rate of 3 °C per minute until it reaches 1200 °C. These conditions assist in ensuring the optimal mechanical properties of the sintered tool. The control of sintering conditions helps in preventing the oxidation, and for uniform temperature distribution. Once at this temperature, the samples are held for 120 min to allow for sintering to occur. After the sintering phase, the furnace power is turned off, but the gas inlet flow remains active. The sintered pellets are then left to cool with the gas flow rate maintained throughout the cooling process. Once the pellets have cooled due to gas flow, then the gas flow is stopped, and the prepared sintered pellets are taken out of the furnace for further processing.

The fabricated cylindrical pellets, now sintered, are cut using a wire EDM machine to obtain the micro EDM tool. Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM) analysis is conducted on the cut rectangular strip of the micro-HEA tool. SEM images at low and high magnification are taken to examine the surface morphology, and Energy-Dispersive X-ray Spectroscopy (EDS) is used to determine the weight percentage of elements present [Fig. 2 ]. In Fig. 2 (b), the EDS analysis shows the elemental composition of high-entropy alloy (HEA) in tool. The mixing entropy of tool is calculated using Equation (1), a method likely specific to the study's context. The mixing entropy values for HEA are obtained from a table presented in Fig. 2(c). The mixing entropy value for the HEA tool is −1.55R. In this context, "R" likely represents the gas constant in thermodynamics. Both mixing entropy values were found to be greater than 1.5R, aligning with the definition of high-entropy alloys (HEAs) [30].

The XRD graph of the AlCoCrFeNi tool was shown in Fig. 2(d) indicates a dual-phase crystal structure. Specifically, it comprises both the phases i.e. FCC (Face-Centered Cubic) and BCC (Body-Centered Cubic). For the FCC phase, peaks are observed at 51.9°, 75.9°, and 92.3°. The above obtained XRD peaks correspond to the (220), (200), and (311) planes, indicates the FCC crystalline structure. This was showing a portion of the alloy espouses a Face-Centered Cubic arrangement. Regarding the BCC phase, peaks are identified at 43.900°, 64.677°, 81.247°, and 97.975°. These peaks correspond to the (110), (200), (211), and (220) planes, indicates the BCC crystalline structure. In short, the XRD pattern dipects a dual-phase crystal structure in the HEA AlCoCrFeNi based micro-tool, with prominent peaks signifying the existence of both FCC and BCC phases.

Electrical Discharge Coating (EDC) is performed by using Micro Electrical Discharge Machining (Micro EDM) (Sparknoix, India) with a powder metallurgy High Entropy Alloy (HEA) tool. In this process, distilled water serves as the dielectric, and when the applied voltage exceeds a certain threshold, sparking occurs between the electrodes, leading to the melting and vaporization of the electrode material [31]. The parameters used in these experiments are crucial, as they directly affect the spark energy and, consequently, the coating properties on the workpiece. Pulse on time being a critical parameter aligns with previous research findings, which emphasize its importance in achieving desirable coating properties. By investigating the effect of pulse on time on these properties, this work contributes to the understanding and optimization of the EDC process. Table 1 provides detailed information about the specific parameter settings used in the experiments conducted for the Micro EDM-based EDC process. Fig. 3 shows the experimental set up used in the present process.

The study employs a variety of characterization techniques to thoroughly investigate the electrical discharge coating (EDC) of high entropy alloy coatings. Square pieces (approximately 10mm × 10mm × 2 mm) are cut and polished to observe the coating layer's morphology and composition. SEM (Model:JEOL, Make: Japan) is used to study morphology and composition. 3D Optical Profilometer (Model: Zygo) quantifies surface roughness, providing detailed information about the texture of the coating. XRD (Bruker AXS Model D8 Dis-cover) is utilized to determine the crystalline structure of the coating. The wettability of the coating is assessed using the sessile drop method with a Drop shape Analyzer (KRUSS DSA 25, Germany). The measurement was performed by following the protocol of Tyagi et al., (2022) which involves observing the behavior of a water droplet on the coating surface (via high-speed camera) and by using 5 μL droplet volume through a syringe of 0.4 m diameter. Corrosion measuring instrument (Model: Kannopy techo solutions and Make: India) is performed to evaluate the corrosion resistance of the coatings. This includes immersion of 5 cm2 workpiece in a 3.5 % sodium chloride (NaCl) solution for 20 h using a three-electrode cell setup. SEM analysis is conducted on the corroded samples to understand the corrosion mechanism. For wear test, we utilized a pin-on-disc test setup, manufactured by MAGNUM. Counter Body of EN31 steel disk was employed. Test Parameters were: rotation Speed: 250 rpm; track diameter: 40 mm, and test duration: 5 min, load: 10N. Samples were polished before the wear test. Coated samples of dimensions 10 mm × 10 mm were pasted with one side of a cylindrical rod measuring 6 mm in diameter and 30 mm in length. Specific Wear Rate (SWR) calculation was done using a formula, which is provided in equation below:

To examine the adhesion pattern of Escherichia coli on both non-coated and coated substrates, protocol of Jain et al. ([32]) with few modifications was used. From the Microbial Type Culture Collection and Gene Bank in India, Escherichia coli (E. coli) culture (MTCC) was acquired The HEA coating and the MgAz31b alloy substrates were used to cultivate bacterial colonies. The viable plate count approach was used for bacterial enumeration [33]. During surface spread method, 6 h incubated sample was at first two time washed with Phosphate Buffer Saline and 1 around 1 ml of inoculum was pipetted out serially diluted in 9 ml of Luria Broth. Serial 10-fold dilutions were made and 0.1 ml of serially diluted sample was spread uniformly on sterile Luria Agar by spreader. Plates were incubated for 6 h at 28 °C temperature and observed the next day for the colony forming units (CFU) per ml.

These methods collectively provide insights into the morphology, topography, elemental composition, crystalline structure, wettability, corrosion behavior, and wear resistance of the high entropy alloy coatings, offering valuable information for further analysis and application considerations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Different phase analysis of HEA coating
ce:section.ce:para: As seen in Fig. 4 , the FCC phase is also present on the HEA-coated surface. The sample S2, which had superior morphology, was the subject of the analysis. It was observed that the Mg sample is highly crystalline, whereas after the coating obtained surfaces has lower crystallinity due to its uniform fine particle size and small grain structure. As a result, the XRD pattern exhibits lower intensity and peak widening due to the presence of smaller grains and particles. The major Mg substrate can be identified by the principal high-intensity peaks in the XRD spectrum since X-rays can only penetrate a certain depth into the sample. In contrast, the polycrystalline coating shows broader peaks with lower intensity, leading to a reduced FCC peak height. FCC Peaks at 43.98, 52.44, 56.99, and 92.89 are identified. These peaks correspond to the (111), (200), (220), and (311) planes, respectively, of the FCC crystalline structure.Li et al. 2022 mentioned that single FCC phase structures are limited and that HEAs are typically observed with dual phase structures [34]. The present coating chemical ordering, elemental segregation and spinodal decomposition in a controlled and efficient method. The FCC crystal structure in the coating is shown in the XRD plot, where each peak represents the FCC phase. The phases that have been discovered align with previous studies on the AlCoCrFeNi HEA [35,36].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Coated surface morphology and chemical composition

ce:section.ce:para:
In Fig. 5, the top-view side morphology of coated magnesium is depicted at different pulse on time and 8A peak current, revealing a discontinuous structure in the coatings. This discontinuity implies the presence of gaps or irregularities in the coating coverage on the pure magnesium surface. Pulse-on Time controls heat input and cooling rate, impelling phase stability. In the current work, a low pulse on time (200 μs) results in a lower energy density, which causes non-uniform or uneven coated surfaces, as Fig. 5 (a, b, c). Very less pulse duration leads to inappropriate melting, causing poor adhesion. On the other hand, an excessively long pulse time causes too much heat input, which raises residual stresses and crack formation. If duration of on time will be consider and increasing the pulse on time values to 400μs results in enhanced coatings with respect to low pulse on time values, as depicted in Fig. 5(d). The 400μs pulse duration minimizes excessive melting and evaporation of coating material. The interface of heat energy, cooling rate, and phase stability impacts the final coat quality. HEA coatings benefit from optimized pulse times that promote phase stability and uniformity. As a result, optimized pulse parameters contribute to uniform elemental distribution by preventing segregation or preferential deposition of specific elements. Debris also plays a role in decreasing microcracks on the recast layer surface, thereby improving its quality. The suspended debris, upon melting in subsequent discharges, moves and attaches to the substrate surface. It contributes to decreasing microcracks on the recast layer surface, thus improving the quality of the coating [37]On further increasing pulse on time again (600 μs), micro-cracks start to increase. The pulse-on time to 800 μs leads to an escalation in void formation, along with widening and elongation of cracks. Therefore, optimum value of pulse on time is required to obtain the voids and cracks free surface. EDS study in Fig. 6 illustrates the uniform distribution of HEA coating. The mixing entropy value for the coating material was 1.56R as calculated from eq (1), confirming the definition of HEA coating. EDS analysis confirms that fraction of each element is similar with a little diffusion of Cr in coating. The microstructure of the deposited HEA-coated surface may alter due to varying processing conditions, thereby influencing the phase constitution [34]. The FCC (Face-Centered Cubic) and BCC (Body-Centered Cubic) phase transitions during the Electrical Discharge Coating (EDC) process of AlCoCrFeNi HEA substantially influence the microstructure of the coating. The fast melting and solidification in EDC offer distinctive phase evolution patterns influenced by temperature and cooling speeds. The FCC dominating structure in S2 coating, as indicated by XRD in Fig. 4
, may result in a smoother, more uniform surface compared to other coatings due to improved molten pool flow and reduced viscosity. Nonetheless, the formation of a BCC dominating structure may result in additional coatings, which can lead to increased cracking due to elevated internal stresses or brittleness, as well as heightened porosity stemming from non-uniform melting or solidification (S1, S3, and S4). The FCC dominating structure is typically linked to fewer defects owing to enhanced ductility, diminished residual stresses, and reduced porosity resulting from improved molten pool fluidity and minimized splashing.

Fig. 7 displays the top-view morphology of coated magnesium (Mg) at different peak current and 400μs pulse on time. This discontinuity indicates the presence of gaps or irregularities in the coating coverage on the pure magnesium surface. At low current levels, the reduced energy density leads to uneven and non-uniform coating deposition, as seen in Fig. 7 (a, b, c). Additionally, lump formation occurs due to debris left in the spark gap, with loosely bonded particles settling on the workpiece surface (Fig. 7(c)). In contrast, higher current values result in improved coating uniformity compared to lower currents (Fig. 7(d)).

Researchers can strategically design surface topographies to control cell behavior without relying on biochemical signals [38]. The study mentions the use of 3D imaging to visualize surface roughness and topography. Fig. 8 and Table 3 provides 3D roughness data obtained by a profilometer, allowing researchers to gain insights into surface features. The 3D optical images in Fig. 8 show variations in surface height, with peaks represented by warmer colors (e.g., red) and valleys by cooler colors (e.g., blue). These variations, or "craters," are likely formed due to localized material deposition and removal during the electrical discharge coating (EDC) process. The Mg surface showed roughness values of 1.3 μm. The roughness values increase after coating, with the highest values observed for sample coating at higher pulse time (Fig. 8). In EDC, higher peak currents result in increased spark energy. This high energy can lead to more intense dissociation of the tool electrode material, which is then deposited onto the substrate surface. Due to the elevated spark energy, deposition occurs rapidly and unevenly. This quick deposition, combined with the high energy, leads to the formation of craters on the substrate, resulting in a rougher surface texture. Moreover, higher Pulse on time result in more intense sparks and increased deposition on the workpiece surface, leading to higher surface roughness. Initially, with a pulse duration of 200 μs, the processed surfaces exhibit minimal void and crack formation, maintaining a relatively low surface roughness of 7.3 μm. Interestingly, while the surface roughness increases to 40.7 μm at a pulse-on time of 400 μs, it subsequently decreases beyond this point. This suggests that there's an optimal pulse duration for minimizing surface roughness, beyond which prolonged exposure may have diminishing returns or even adverse effects on surface quality. Therefore, surface roughness increases after the deposition of the HEA coating on Mg surfaces, highlighting the importance of optimizing pulse on time to achieve uniform coating formation and desired surface characteristics.

The average coating thickness (ACT) was determined by measuring at five different points. The coating layer in EDC typically forms due to the solidification process of particles that haven't been completely evacuated. These particles migrate from both the sintered tool and the dielectric materials during the coating procedure. Elements from the dielectric and electrode material combine to form a robust and HEA layer on the treated surface. At 400 μs duration, the thickness is found to be 159.3 μs as shown in Fig. 9 (a). EDS analysis at interface of coating confirms that fraction of each element is similar with a little diffusion of Fe (Fig. 9(b)). The mixing entropy value of coating layer as per table in Fig. 9(c) was −1.62R, confirming the formation of HEA coating.

This section discusses the electrochemical corrosion properties of the AlCoCrFeNi coating in a 3.5 % NaCl solution, with a comparison to the Mg AZ31B base material for reference. Sample 2, prepared at a high pulse on time, exhibited better morphology, prompting the corrosion test to be conducted specifically on this coating over various durations. The corrosion properties were evaluated using the tafle method, with results presented in Fig. 10 and quantitative corrosion values listed in Table 2 . The electrochemical theory was employed for assessment, where a more positive free corrosion potential (Ecorr) and a smaller free corrosion current density (Icorr) indicate better corrosion resistance. The AlCoCrFeNi coating demonstrated higher Ecorr and lower Icorr values compared to the base material in the 3.5 % NaCl solution, suggesting superior corrosion resistance. All the coated sample showed lower CPR than MgAZ21b. The minimum Icorr value plays important role in achieving the lowest CPR value which is 0.16 mm/year for S2. Researchers revealed that AlCoCrFeNi HEA coatings exhibit superior corrosion and wear resistance relative to traditional coatings, including hard chrome, WC-Co, and Ni-based coatings. Stable oxide layers (Al2O3 and Cr2O3) augment corrosion resistance, whereas their elevated hardness and microstructural stability promote wear resistance [39]. Santosh et al. (2019) demonstrated that hard chromium coatings have excellent wear resistance; nonetheless, they are susceptible to micro-cracking, which might serve as loci for corrosion start, hence undermining their protective efficacy [40]. Moreover, WC-Co coatings provide significant hardness; yet, they may exhibit brittleness and are prone to oxidation at high temperatures, resulting in diminished wear and corrosion resistance [41]. While Ni-based coatings demonstrate satisfactory corrosion resistance, their efficacy may decline in harsh settings, necessitating further alloying or post-treatments to improve their wear characteristics. Formation of protective oxide layers enhances structural stability, hence prolonging the service life of components in demanding applications. The corrosion resistance of high-entropy alloys (HEAs) is significantly affected by their microstructural phase composition (FCC versus BCC) [42,43]. To enhance corrosion resistance in AlCoCrFeNi high-entropy alloy (HEA) coatings, the objective is to encourage FCC phase formation while reducing BCC phase and intermetallic, which can exacerbate localized corrosion. The FCC dominating structure allows dense, high atomic packing, resulting in a stable Cr/Ni/Co solid solution and enhanced corrosion resistance due to uniform elemental distribution and the creation of a stable passive layer. At low pulse-on durations, a development of predominant BCC structures may occur, as evidenced in HEA alloys. This dual-phase structure may lead to phase separation and the development of σ-phase, B2-phase, and carbides, which can serve as sites for corrosion initiation [43]. The existence of many phases with distinct electrochemical potentials promotes localized corrosion. The uneven distribution of Cr and Al in BCC structures may result in discontinuous or faulty oxide layers. At an optimum pulse duration of 400μs, the predominant FCC solid solution phase minimizes phase separation and guarantees a more homogeneous elemental distribution, as elements such as Cr and Al establish a stable Cr2O3/Al2O3 passive layer, therefore decreasing corrosion susceptibility.

SEM images in Fig. 11 shows that the Mg workpiece exhibited the highest number of rust spots, with a decreasing trend observed for both Mg and coated samples. This indicates that the coatings effectively reduce the formation of rust spots and cracks compared to the bare Mg workpiece. A lower corrosion wear rate implies slower corrosion or wear of the coating material over time in a corrosive environment. The data suggest that the fabricated coatings have significantly lower corrosion wear rates compared to the bare Mg workpiece. Overall, the observations suggest that the fabricated coatings effectively reduce both the occurrence of rust spots and the corrosion wear rate compared to the uncoated Mg workpiece. This indicates the potential of the coatings to enhance the corrosion resistance and durability of surfaces, particularly titanium surfaces. The presence of chromium (Cr) and aluminum (Al) in the AlCoCrFeNi coating contributes significantly to its excellent corrosion resistance. These elements play a key role in improving the resistance of the alloy coating against corrosion. Also, surface roughness and higher contact angle is primary factor in determining the corrosion [44]. The roughness of the coating creates valleys that can trap air. This trapped air acts as a passive barrier, preventing aggressive ions, like chlorine ions in NaCl solutions, from reaching the underlying uncoated substrate. Essentially, the air-filled valleys act as a shield. By reducing the contact area between the rough surface and the corrosive electrolyte, the air pockets limit the exposure of the coating to the NaCl solution. This restriction minimizes the pathways for chlorine ions to penetrate, thereby slowing down the corrosion process. [45]. In this way, the roughness of the surface not only improves corrosion resistance mechanically but also through the creation of these protective air pockets, which serve as a barrier to corrosive elements. Moreover, chromium present in the coating when exposed to oxygen, quickly reacts to form a stable and adherent chromium oxide film. This oxide layer is extremely thin but dense, which acts as a barrier between the underlying metal and the environment, thereby preventing further oxidation and corrosion [46].

Contact angle measurement is a common technique to assess surface wetting properties. A higher contact angle indicates greater hydrophobicity, while a lower contact angle suggests greater hydrophilicity. Fig. 12 shows that Mg surface has low roughness, resulting in a hydrophilic state with a contact angle of 58.7°. After applying the high entropy alloy (HEA) coating, surface roughness increases significantly, leading to a higher contact angle. According to Wenzel's model, an increase in surface roughness corresponds with a rise in contact angle. The distinct topographical characteristics of a coated surface, including craters, valleys, and changes in roughness, substantially impact its wettability by altering the interaction between liquids and the surface. A more detailed investigation of specific topographical features, including as craters, valleys, and micro-pits, and their impact on wettability is crucial for a comprehensive understanding. The Electrical Discharge Coating (EDC) technique shapes surface morphology by fast melting, solidification, and material transfer. The high-energy electrical discharge results in crater-like depressions that can either retain liquid (increasing adhesion in a hydrophilic context) or form air pockets (decreasing wettability in a hydrophobic context). These micro-voids can augment the surface area, resulting in enhanced roughness and potentially elevating the perceived contact angle. Excessive porosity may result in localized wetting instabilities. The hardened molten substance creates a coarse, microstructured recast layer that directly influences the interaction between the liquid and the coated surface. Wenzel's model presupposes total liquid infiltration into surface asperities; nevertheless, when air pockets are entrapped, the system may diverge from the Wenzel regime and adhere to the Cassie-Baxter model instead. As air pockets develop within surface valleys or craters, the liquid fails to completely infiltrate the irregular features, resulting in an elevated contact angle and increased hydrophobicity according to the Cassie and Baxter model. Extensive craters may generate localized areas of heightened roughness, potentially entraping air or liquid, contingent upon the surface chemistry. Deep valleys enhance the solid-liquid contact area, facilitating hydrophilicity, whereas shallow valleys with air entrapment foster hydrophobicity. In EDC, deposited particles on the coated surface may induce heterogeneity, potentially modifying the wetting behavior by disturbing uniform contact.

For better study, Table 3
shows 3D roughness, CPR and contact angle for coating prepared at different pulse on time. Increased roughness provides more surface area for liquid interaction, leading to a higher contact angle. The addition of HEA may exacerbate surface irregularities, leading to increased surface openings and higher roughness. Consequently, the contact angle also increases due to the larger surface area available for liquid interaction. Hydrophobicity tends to increase with increasing corrosion resistance or decreasing corrosion penetration rate (CPR). A hydrophobic surface creates a barrier against corrosive mediums, inhibiting the corrosion process. The hydrophobic nature of surfaces, indicated by a high contact angle, can be beneficial for various biological functions, as hydrophobic interactions contribute to stability, particularly in protein interactions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dry sliding wear test pin on disk study
ce:section.ce:para: According to Fig. 13 , the addition of HEA had a positive effect on specific wear rate (SWR), and coefficient of friction (COF). For the HEA coating, SWR ranged from 0.00137 g/N-m to 0.000145 g/N-m, whereas it was 0.00178 g/N-m for the MgAZ31b surface. Specifically, the SWR for the HEA for S2 (with 400μs Pulse on time) was at its minimum, even lower than that of the reference material. This suggests that less material was removed from the HEA coating as compared to workpiece. In Fig. 14 (b), scratches in S2 were observed in the sliding direction, indicating oxide formation, as confirmed by XRD in Fig. 14 (d). The plastic deformation within wear tracks was higher for sample prepared at lower pulse on time (S1). Furthermore, the FF of HEA coating (S1) initially rose to approximately 2.9 N within the first 40 s due to the initiation of the stick-slip process. However, after some time, a constant value of approximately 1.7 N was maintained for the long-term test. In contrast, for Mg surface, there was an initial drop followed by a continuous rise in FF, reaching 4.9 N. Compared to the other coated sample, the S1 wear surface exhibited large-scale debris, indicative of severe adhesive wear. This behavior can be attributed to the characteristics of the alloy, where the BCC (Body-Centered Cubic) phase in AlFeCrCoNi HEA possesses higher hardness, while the FCC (Face-Centered Cubic) phase exhibits good plasticity [47]. It is presumed that the BCC phase bears the main load during the test, leading to crack formation, while the FCC phase may promote crack propagation. The crack-induced peeling area of the coating was susceptible to damage, forming debris particles as indicated by the SEM for sample S1 suggesting the presence of abrasive wear in dry conditions. As previously noted, BCC dominating phases linked to shortened pulse on time (S1 coating) exhibit increased brittleness at lower temperatures, rendering them vulnerable to breaking under strong impact or cyclic loading, which results in diminished wear resistance in certain applications. The FCC dominating structure established at a 400μs pulse duration (S2 coating) in austenitic stainless steels and nickel-based alloys demonstrates enhanced ductility and toughness, facilitating impact absorption and fracture propagation resistance during wear. Prolonged heating at 600 μs and 800 μs may facilitate a minor increase in BCC phase transformation due to enhanced atomic diffusion, resulting in a negligible reduction in wear resistance. Single-phase coatings with body-centered cubic (BCC) or face-centered cubic (FCC) lattices have superior wear resistance compared to dual-phase coatings at ambient temperature. [48,49]. The condition facilitates the precipitation of intermetallic compounds (σ-phase, B2-phase, Laves phase). The Pulse-On Time of 400μs (Balanced Heat Input and Cooling Rate) facilitates the creation of FCC phases, resulting in a single-phase microstructure that enhances hardness by inhibiting the development of excessive brittle intermetallic. The creation of the Al2O3 layer significantly affects the friction and wear characteristics of the alloy, especially at high temperatures [50]. A thin layer of aluminium oxide (Al2O3) develops on the outermost surface of the oxide layer, serving as a protective barrier that prevents additional oxidation of the alloy. This improves the alloy's resistance to oxidation. In Mg AZ31b alloy, SEM images revealed that these blocks had a high concentration of debris of oxygen and magnesium, indicating the formation of magnesium oxide. The worn surface for Mg showed deep grooves, fractured surface and large number of debris. In contrast, HEA coating for S2 shows abrasion in sliding direction. This suggests that the presence of HEA particles may contribute to the observed low SWR, FF, and COF. Overall, the addition of HEA improves the wear resistance and friction behavior of Mg surface, making them potentially suitable for applications where these properties are crucial (see Fig. 13).

The XRD analysis conducted on the debris particles after the tribology test provides valuable insights into the elemental phases formed during the test (Fig. 14). The peaks observed in the XRD plot indicate the presence of Al, P, Si, and O. These findings suggest that the wear debris originated from both the disc and composite pin. The highest intensity peak identified in the XRD plot indicates the formation of Silicon oxide (SiO2) of the EN31 disk, as detailed in table in Fig. 14 (e). This suggests that the material worn from the disk during sliding motion may also adheres to the composite pin in the form of wear debris. This observation supports the conclusion that the disk experienced higher material removal compared to the pin. Specifically, the presence of peaks corresponding to Silicon Oxide (SiO2), and Aluminum Phosphate further validates the mechanism of wear due to oxide formation and the transfer of debris between the disk and pin surfaces.

The investigation sought to compare the bacterial load on magnesium-coated surfaces with that on uncoated surfaces utilizing the viable plate count method. The CFU were assessed for both surface types. The findings demonstrate that the magnesium-coated surfaces consistently displayed a reduced bacterial count in comparison to the non-coated surfaces (Table 4 and Fig. 15 ). The average CFU/mL for magnesium-coated surfaces was 87× 10^4, but the average CFU/mL for non-coated surfaces was substantially more at 133 x 10^4. The reduced CFU/mL noted on the magnesium-coated surfaces indicates that the magnesium coating possesses antibacterial characteristics that significantly inhibit bacterial colonization [51]. This suggests the efficacy of magnesium coatings in scenarios requiring the reduction of bacterial contamination. Hydrophobicity significantly influences protein adsorption onto surfaces, hence affecting cell adhesion, immunological response, and overall biocompatibility. Although hydrophobic surfaces can facilitate protein attachment, they may impede cell adhesion if the protein layer undergoes denaturation. Coatings with regulated hydrophobicity on implants can be employed to refine protein interactions, enhancing tissue integration and reducing bacterial colonization [52]. The viable plate count method indicated that magnesium-coated surfaces contain markedly less germs compared to non-coated surfaces. This outcome substantiates the assertion that HEA coatings can diminish bacterial presence on diverse surfaces, hence potentially improving hygiene and mitigating the risk of bacterial infections on medical devices.

The summary encapsulates the main findings from the characterization study of the AlCoCrFeNi (HEA) coating prepared on MgAZ31B workpieces using the electrical discharge coating (EDC) process. Here's a breakdown of the key conclusions.
1.
The XRD spectrum reveals the presence of both body-centered cubic (BCC) and face-centered cubic (FCC) phases in the coated sample, indicating a crystalline structure with arrangements of both BCC and FCC crystallographic structures. Notably, the intensity peak associated with the FCC phase decreases while that of the BCC phase increases.
2.
Optimal pulse on time values (e.g., 400μs) result in favorable inter-metallic bonding between the workpiece and coating powders, minimizing defects at the intersection region. SEM observations at the cross-section of the coated surface show the presence of voids and large pores, particularly at low levels of pulse on time, suggesting that adjusting the pulse on time to higher levels can improve the coating quality.
3.
Coating at higher pulse on time leads to intense and deeper material deposition, resulting in higher surface roughness due to pronounced irregularities. The increase in roughness aligns with Wenzel's model, indicating that higher roughness increases the contact angle. The HEA coating with maximum roughness exhibits a contact angle of 95.8° compared to 58.7° for the Mg workpiece.
4.
The addition of HEA (High-Entropy Alloy) had a beneficial impact on both the specific wear rate (SWR) and coefficient of friction (COF). Specifically, the SWR for the HEA coating ranged from 0.00137 g/N-m to 0.000145 g/N-m, indicating a lower rate of wear compared to the MgAZ31b surface, which had a SWR of 0.00178 g/N-m. This improvement in wear resistance and friction characteristics could be advantageous in Mg implant applications.
5.
The coated sample exhibits a significantly lower corrosion penetration rate in salt solution compared to the bare Mg substrate, indicating superior corrosion protection provided by the coating. This is attributed to the passivation phenomenon, indicating excellent chemical robustness, stability, and corrosion protection, especially in saline environments.
6.
Despite expectations, the HEA coating exhibited different surface morphology and cell interactions than anticipated. This suggests that the HEA coating may possess unique properties or interactions with cells that were not fully understood or predicted.
7.
By addressing the rapid degradation issue, magnesium alloys have the potential to become a standard material for biodegradable implants in various medical applications, such as bone fixation devices, cardiovascular stents, and tissue scaffolds.
